paragraphs.en: Welcome to Signals and Threads, in-depth conversations about every layer of the tech stack from Jane Street. I'm your host, Ron Minsky. Today I'm talking with Andy Ray about programmable hardware.
paragraphs.zh: 欢迎收听 Signals and Threads，这是来自 Jane Street 的深度技术对话节目，涵盖技术栈的每一层。我是主持人 Ron Minsky。今天我将与 Andy Ray 聊聊可编程硬件。

paragraphs.en: Hello, Ron, good to be chatting with you.
paragraphs.zh: 你好，Ron，很高兴能和你聊天。

paragraphs.en: Andy is a longtime veteran of the hardware industry. He spent over a decade building real shippable hardware at companies like Xilinx and STMicroelectronics before coming to Jane Street. And at Jane Street he's been doing hardware work ever since, including building Hardcaml, a library for building hardware in OCaml. Today I want to talk with him about both what's interesting about hardware as a programming challenge, and about Hardcaml as a way of building hardware.
paragraphs.zh: Andy 是硬件行业的资深老将。在加入 Jane Street 之前，他在 Xilinx 和意法半导体等公司花了十多年时间打造真正可量产的硬件。在 Jane Street，他一直从事硬件工作，其中包括构建 Hardcaml——一个用 OCaml 编写硬件的库。今天我想和他聊聊硬件作为一种编程挑战有什么有趣之处，以及 Hardcaml 作为硬件构建方式的意义。

paragraphs.en: Sure. So hardware allows you to build customized architectures for a specific problem, which can be faster and more efficient than software on a general purpose CPU. At the same time, it can do this while running many operations in parallel. So, you know, you might be running a billion operations a second, and each of those a billion operations could themselves consist of many highly parallel sub-operations all going on at the same time. So even though a, you know, a CPU of several gigahertz is itself quite fast, you know, hardware can be even significantly faster.
paragraphs.zh: 当然。硬件允许你为特定问题构建定制化的架构，这可以比通用 CPU 上的软件更快、更高效。与此同时，它还能在并行运行大量操作的同时做到这一点。你知道，你可能每秒执行十亿次操作，而这十亿次操作中的每一次本身又可以由许多高度并行的子操作同时进行。所以即便一个运行在几吉赫兹的 CPU 本身已经相当快了，硬件还是可以快得多。

paragraphs.en: That description on the face of it sounds awesome. And in fact, it sounds from what you said so far, like hardware is just a pure win, like it's faster, it's more parallel. Why would you use software at all?
paragraphs.zh: 从表面上看，这个描述听起来很棒。事实上，从你目前所说的来看，硬件似乎就是纯粹的赢家——更快、更并行。那为什么还要用软件呢？

paragraphs.en: Oh, my goodness, yes, there are a lot. So it is fundamentally this: hardware designs are much, much more expensive and time-consuming to produce. Like, you know, writing software in a day, you might produce many, many functions of useful code. Writing hardware in a day, you might produce one, you know, relatively small piece of useful hardware. And it gets worse than that, because it's also slower to simulate and verify. And it is hard to change once you've submitted.
paragraphs.zh: 哦，天哪，当然有很多原因。根本原因在于：硬件设计的生产成本远远更高，耗时也远远更长。比如，写软件一天可能写出很多很多有用的函数。写硬件一天，你可能只能做出一个相对较小的有用硬件模块。而且情况还不止如此，因为硬件的仿真和验证也更慢。而且一旦提交，就很难更改。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. And just to interrupt for a second though, that was a bit of terminology that really confused me when I first started interacting with hardware people. You often hear the term "intellectual property" or IP, and I was really confused about what that meant in this context. Can you just explain what IP is?
paragraphs.zh: 嗯。不过先打断一下，这是一个在我刚开始和硬件人打交道时让我非常困惑的术语。你经常会听到「知识产权」或 IP 这个词，我当时真的搞不清楚在这个语境下它是什么意思。你能解释一下 IP 是什么吗？

paragraphs.en: Correct.
paragraphs.zh: 对。

paragraphs.en: Which is to say some component that somebody else wrote that you get to integrate. Except in this case, it's not an API, it's actual gate-level descriptions or RTL descriptions of a circuit.
paragraphs.zh: 也就是说，是别人写的某个组件，你可以将其集成进来。只不过在这种情况下，它不是 API，而是电路的实际门级描述或 RTL 描述。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, that's right. I don't know why that terminology came about, but it's just always been called IP. And typically, what you do is you buy licenses to use IPs and then incorporate them into your design.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，没错。我不知道这个术语是怎么来的，但它一直就被叫做 IP。通常的做法是购买使用 IP 的许可证，然后将其集成到你的设计中。

paragraphs.en: Right, I think of that as one of the paradoxes of hardware: hardware is in the micro, in many different ways, more expressive than software. And yet in the macro, hardware designs are quite inflexible relative to software designs in the sense that they're very hard to modify. And in particular, that's a consequence of the fact that the abstraction barrier in hardware is much weaker than in software.
paragraphs.zh: 对，我认为这是硬件的悖论之一：在微观层面，硬件在许多方面比软件更具表达力。然而在宏观层面，硬件设计相比软件设计相当不灵活——它们很难修改。尤其是，这是由于硬件中的抽象屏障远比软件中的弱所导致的。

paragraphs.en: Yes.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。

paragraphs.en: The thing that you're generating in a hardware design is essentially some layout of the circuit, the physical substrate. And when you design things in software, you are much more insulated from the physical substrate.
paragraphs.zh: 你在硬件设计中生成的东西本质上是电路的某种布局，即物理基底。而在软件中进行设计时，你与物理基底的隔离程度要高得多。

paragraphs.en: It is and unfortunately, the abstraction tools we have, they take us some way. So you know, you talk about things in terms of, you know, registers and wires and logic gates and such. And you can work through those abstractions and, and you can build bigger and bigger things in that way. But you'll never be fully insulated from the hardware below.
paragraphs.zh: 确实如此，但遗憾的是，我们拥有的抽象工具只能带我们走一部分路。你知道，你用寄存器、导线、逻辑门这些术语来描述事物。你可以在这些抽象之上工作，并以这种方式构建越来越大的东西。但你永远无法完全与底层硬件隔离。

paragraphs.en: So you were talking there about ASICS and FPGAs. Can you just quickly explain what those are.
paragraphs.zh: 你刚才提到了 ASIC 和 FPGA。你能简单解释一下它们是什么吗？

paragraphs.en: An ASIC is a custom-made chip that can perform a single function. In contrast, an FPGA is a reprogrammable chip. So the hardware circuitry inside the FPGA can be configured to implement any digital circuit. And that's achieved through, you know, a sea of reconfigurable logic elements all joined together.
paragraphs.zh: ASIC 是一种可以执行单一功能的定制芯片。相比之下，FPGA 是一种可重新编程的芯片。FPGA 内部的硬件电路可以被配置为实现任何数字电路。这是通过大量互联在一起的可重配置逻辑单元来实现的。

paragraphs.en: Got it. So when you talked about what the advantages are of hardware, you talked about how by having a customized design, you could achieve massive parallelism and you could avoid the slowdowns that come from having general purpose hardware running general purpose software. But is there another advantage that we sort of haven't talked about directly, which is something about timing and determinism?
paragraphs.zh: 明白了。那么当你谈到硬件的优势时，你提到了通过定制化设计可以实现大规模并行，并且可以避免通用硬件运行通用软件所带来的性能下降。但还有一个我们没有直接谈到的优势，那就是关于时序和确定性的问题？

paragraphs.en: It's obviously going to depend on the sort of problem you're trying to solve. But you know, an area where it really matters is network processing, where you have to process network data, you know, at some fixed rate, many gigabits per second or even hundreds of gigabits or even terabits per second, to look at every single packet on the network, and to do some computation on the data of each packet.
paragraphs.zh: 这显然取决于你试图解决的问题类型。但你知道，一个真正重要的领域是网络处理，在这里你必须以固定速率处理网络数据——每秒数吉比特，甚至数百吉比特，乃至太比特——查看网络上的每一个数据包，并对每个数据包的数据进行某种计算。

paragraphs.en: Right, so-called "deep packet inspection" is the term of art here?
paragraphs.zh: 对，这里的专业术语就是所谓的「深度包检测」？

paragraphs.en: Yep. And you know, there are absolutely gigantic hardware designs which ingress hundreds of network inputs, look at every single bit of data, and respond. You know, and respond with some decision in well under a microsecond.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。你知道，确实存在一些极其庞大的硬件设计，它们接收数百路网络输入，查看每一位数据，并在远远不到一微秒的时间内作出响应，给出某种决策。

paragraphs.en: Part of the advantage is there's like an order of magnitude or more improvement in the bulk throughput of the thing. But there's also a timing argument. Is that right? There's something about the predictability of the timing of hardware that's useful.
paragraphs.zh: 其中一部分优势是整体吞吐量提升了一个数量级甚至更多。但还有一个关于时序的论点，对吗？硬件时序的可预测性有某种有用之处。

paragraphs.en: Yeah.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。

paragraphs.en: Which is to say, you can build one of these designs, such that it can simply consume everything that comes in on the network, with nothing dropped and with a very consistent latency through the system, in a way that you simply couldn't achieve with software.
paragraphs.zh: 也就是说，你可以构建这样一种设计，让它能够接收网络上进来的所有数据，不丢弃任何内容，并且系统中的延迟非常一致——这是用软件根本无法实现的。

paragraphs.en: That's true, and I think there's a problem that we have to deal with here at Jane Street a lot, and it's a real challenge. Software is nondeterministic in many ways, right, and the best way, really, to get rid of nondeterminism in software is to run hardware.
paragraphs.zh: 确实如此，而且我认为这是我们在 Jane Street 经常要处理的问题，是一个真正的挑战。软件在许多方面都存在不确定性，对吧，而消除软件中不确定性的最佳方式，其实就是运行硬件。

paragraphs.en: You mentioned that some of the ways of getting rid of the nondeterminism involve tweaking your operating system settings and writing your software very carefully. In what ways is hardware fundamentally more deterministic than software?
paragraphs.zh: 你提到消除不确定性的一些方法包括调整操作系统设置和非常谨慎地编写软件。那么硬件在哪些方面从根本上比软件更具确定性？

paragraphs.en: Yeah, that's generally true. I mean I should say that there is nondeterminism possible in hardware designs. But the default thing when you turn on a hardware design is for it to be completely deterministic. You know, every set of input signals produces a known and predictable output. So you know, given the same inputs at the same clock, the same outputs will always appear.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，总体上是这样。我应该说，硬件设计中也可能存在不确定性。但当你启动一个硬件设计时，默认情况下它是完全确定的。你知道，每一组输入信号都会产生已知且可预测的输出。所以，给定相同的输入在相同的时钟下，总是会出现相同的输出。

paragraphs.en: In some sense, it's an issue of what the defaults are, the core language that you're working in when you write hardware at the level of gates and registers, is inherently, by its nature, a synchronous language. And in a synchronous language, things are deterministic by default, as opposed to the software world.
paragraphs.zh: 从某种意义上说，这是关于默认行为的问题。当你在门和寄存器层面编写硬件时所使用的核心语言，本质上就是一种同步语言。在同步语言中，事物默认是确定的，这与软件世界恰恰相反。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, that's right.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，没错。

paragraphs.en: So this overall story of why hardware is different and why it's useful and why it lets you achieve goals you couldn't achieve with software is interesting and important. But I think we should also dig in a little deeper on what the actual things are that hardware engineers work with, and what the tools look like. So can you give me a deeper description of what an FPGA is and what an ASIC is?
paragraphs.zh: 关于硬件为何不同、为何有用、以及为何能让你实现软件无法实现的目标，这整体的故事既有趣又重要。但我认为我们还应该深入了解一下硬件工程师实际使用的东西，以及工具是什么样的。你能更深入地描述一下 FPGA 和 ASIC 是什么吗？

paragraphs.en: Yes. So let's start with an FPGA. It stands for field programmable gate array. An FPGA consists of a large array of logic elements. And typically an FPGA has somewhere between ten thousand and ten million logic elements in it. But they're all programmable. So you load a design into an FPGA, by programming those logic elements, and once programmed they do a specific computation in hardware. To reprogram an FPGA, you simply load a new design.
paragraphs.zh: 好的。先从 FPGA 说起。它代表现场可编程门阵列。FPGA 由大量逻辑单元阵列组成。通常一个 FPGA 内部有一万到一千万个逻辑单元。但它们都是可编程的。你通过对这些逻辑单元编程来将设计加载到 FPGA 中，一旦编程完成，它们就会在硬件中执行特定的计算。要重新编程 FPGA，只需加载一个新设计即可。

paragraphs.en: And, by the way, you've been using the term, ASIC. I understand what an FPGA is. What is an ASIC?
paragraphs.zh: 顺便问一下，你一直在用 ASIC 这个术语。我理解 FPGA 是什么了。那 ASIC 是什么？

paragraphs.en: So, it stands for application specific integrated circuit. It's the term we tend to use for hardware that is in some sense is fixed into silicon, as opposed to an FPGA where the logic elements are reconfigurable. So when we talk about an ASIC, we mean chip design which once cast in silicon does not change.
paragraphs.zh: 它代表专用集成电路。这是我们通常用来指代某种意义上固化在硅片中的硬件的术语，与 FPGA 中逻辑单元可重配置的情况相对。所以当我们谈到 ASIC 时，我们指的是一旦铸入硅片就不会改变的芯片设计。

paragraphs.en: So it sounds like the key advantage of FPGAs is that they are reconfigurable.
paragraphs.zh: 所以听起来 FPGA 的主要优势在于它们是可重配置的。

paragraphs.en: They are.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。

paragraphs.en: What do you lose for that? In what way are ASICs better than FPGAs?
paragraphs.zh: 为此你失去了什么？ASIC 在哪些方面优于 FPGA？

paragraphs.en: On basically every performance front ASICs are superior. Like the power that an ASIC will use could be orders of magnitude less for the equivalent computation. And they will also run considerably faster and are considerably denser. So those are actually really big differences. And FPGA can actually be quite slow and use quite a lot of power. And the reason for that is that all of the reconfigurability of the FPGA has a cost in terms of the amount of chip area that's needed to implement the design.
paragraphs.zh: 在基本上所有性能方面，ASIC 都更优越。比如，ASIC 在执行等效计算时的功耗可能要低几个数量级。它们的运行速度也会快得多，密度也大得多。所以这些实际上是非常大的差异。而 FPGA 实际上可能相当慢，耗电量也相当大。原因在于 FPGA 的所有可重配置性都需要付出代价——需要更多的芯片面积来实现设计。

paragraphs.en: Right, and then the economics are in your favor, and that the cost per unit is much, much smaller than if you were paying for an FPGA per unit.
paragraphs.zh: 对，然后经济账就对你有利了——每单位成本远远低于按单位购买 FPGA 的情况。

paragraphs.en: Oh, yeah, yeah.
paragraphs.zh: 哦，是的，确实。

paragraphs.en: The economics of this is all very interesting in the sense that, like, one thing one can be struck by is the enormous cost of creating an ASIC in the first place. Can you describe what the economics are here?
paragraphs.zh: 这方面的经济学非常有趣，比如，一个让人印象深刻的地方就是最初创建一个 ASIC 的巨大成本。你能描述一下这里的经济逻辑吗？

paragraphs.en: They really do.
paragraphs.zh: 确实如此。

paragraphs.en: But if you want to have a small number of them, there's no comparison, it's way better to get a small production run of ASICs if you can, or build your system on top of FPGAs.
paragraphs.zh: 但如果你只需要少量的话，就没有可比性了——如果可以的话，小批量生产 ASIC 会好得多，或者在 FPGA 上构建你的系统。

paragraphs.en: Even, like, the economics of just FPGAs is really interesting, actually. If I went to try and buy the FPGAs that we use at Jane Street in ones, they'd be around $10,000 each. But if you were a big player and bought them in large quantities, I know some of these sell for around $300 each. Yeah.
paragraphs.zh: 其实，光是 FPGA 本身的经济学就很有意思。如果我试着单个购买我们在 Jane Street 使用的 FPGA，每个大约要一万美元。但如果你是大客户，大批量采购的话，我知道有些 FPGA 每个大约卖三百美元。是的。

paragraphs.en: Wow.
paragraphs.zh: 哇。

paragraphs.en: An absolutely outrageous difference. It's all built into them, you know, because they're their customer, we are not their customer. They sell billions of these things to big manufacturers and they price accordingly, you know, and the price to an individual doing their own design is commensurate with the difficulty of actually doing it yourself.
paragraphs.zh: 这差价真的是离谱。这都是内置在他们的定价体系里的，因为大厂商才是他们的客户，我们不是。他们向大型制造商销售数十亿个这样的产品，定价也相应如此。对于自己做设计的个人来说，价格与你自己实际做这件事的难度是相称的。

paragraphs.en: So let's switch gears a little, I'd like to actually just understand a little bit more about your own history with hardware, and just what it's like to actually do hardware design day to day. So can you give me a brief description of how you got into hardware and what's interesting about it?
paragraphs.zh: 那我们换个话题，我想多了解一点你自己与硬件的经历，以及日常做硬件设计是什么感觉。你能简单描述一下你是怎么进入硬件行业的，以及它有什么有趣之处吗？

paragraphs.en: So the first time I was ever introduced to hardware was at university, I did a computer science and electronics degree. And you know, I wrote a few things in Verilog to do some coursework, but that was it. And then later in my career, I got a job at a company where I started doing hardware designs in Verilog. And to be honest, I fell in love with it instantly. I thought it was a really beautiful thing to do.
paragraphs.zh: 我第一次接触硬件是在大学，我学的是计算机科学和电子学双学位。你知道，我用 Verilog 写了一些东西来完成课程作业，但也就止于此了。然后在我职业生涯的后期，我在一家公司找到了一份工作，开始用 Verilog 做硬件设计。说实话，我立刻就爱上了它。我认为这是一件非常美好的事情。

paragraphs.en: So there's, there's obvious delight in your voice describing this work, and I'm curious, what is it about hardware that you love?
paragraphs.zh: 在你描述这项工作时，声音里明显流露出喜悦，我很好奇，你喜欢硬件的什么？

paragraphs.en: I should say, I enjoy writing software as well. But I do prefer writing hardware. And I think it's the combination of working on a thing that is more abstract in the sense that, you know, you're not doing sequential things and you're not doing object-oriented things. Rather, you're thinking about and dealing with graphs and signals flowing from registers to other registers, and you're doing it all simultaneously. I find that a particularly beautiful way to think and code and design stuff.
paragraphs.zh: 我应该说，我也喜欢写软件。但我确实更喜欢写硬件。我认为这是因为处理的东西在某种意义上更抽象——你不是在做顺序的事情，也不是在做面向对象的事情。相反，你在思考和处理图以及从寄存器流向其他寄存器的信号，而且你是同时进行所有这些工作的。我觉得这是一种特别美妙的思考、编码和设计方式。

paragraphs.en: Right, it's like this kind of graph structured computation that you have floating around in your head, and you're essentially composing these graphs and you're doing it in a way that everything happens at the same time.
paragraphs.zh: 对，就像脑子里浮现着一种图结构的计算，你本质上是在组合这些图，而且以一种所有事情同时发生的方式来完成。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, something like that. Something like that. The models are kind of interesting. So as you sort of learn how to design hardware, you learn about timing analysis and how to make your circuit run as fast as possible. And it really does feel like a puzzle, a really interesting combinatorial puzzle of trying to think about things happening simultaneously and maximizing the efficiency of the circuit.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，大概是这样。就是这种感觉。其中的思维模型很有意思。当你学习如何设计硬件时，你会学习时序分析以及如何让你的电路尽可能快地运行。这真的感觉像是一个谜题，一个非常有趣的组合优化谜题——试图思考同时发生的事情并最大化电路的效率。

paragraphs.en: So that's how you got into this business of doing hardware. So what led you from there to start experimenting with building hardware in OCaml, which is really kind of the main topic of Hardcaml?
paragraphs.zh: 所以这就是你进入硬件这行的经历。那是什么让你从那里开始尝试用 OCaml 构建硬件的呢？这实际上是 Hardcaml 的核心话题。

paragraphs.en: It was frustration with the tools that we were using to build hardware, in particular, testing stuff. The existing hardware languages, the traditional hardware languages do not have great testing tools. And they're also not great general purpose programming languages. So for me, it felt like something of a step back to use those languages compared to the kind of languages I could use in software. So I started experimenting with F# first.
paragraphs.zh: 是对我们用来构建硬件的工具的挫败感，特别是测试方面。现有的硬件语言，那些传统的硬件语言，没有很好的测试工具。它们也不是很好的通用编程语言。所以对我来说，与我在软件中可以使用的语言相比，使用这些语言感觉像是一种倒退。于是我首先开始用 F# 进行实验。

paragraphs.en: That is a true fact. Although people are working on this now.
paragraphs.zh: 这确实是事实。虽然现在已经有人在努力改进这一点了。

paragraphs.en: I know they are. So I used F#, I liked F# actually. And then I managed to get switched over to Linux, and that's when OCaml really opened up as an option, because there was no OCaml on Windows at that time. So I started experimenting with OCaml, and I just preferred it over F#. And so that turned into what became Hardcaml.
paragraphs.zh: 我知道。所以我用了 F#，其实我挺喜欢 F# 的。后来我设法切换到了 Linux，这时 OCaml 才真正成为一个选项，因为那时候 Windows 上没有 OCaml。于是我开始用 OCaml 实验，我就是比起 F# 更喜欢它。就这样，它演变成了后来的 Hardcaml。

paragraphs.en: So it sounds like part of the advantage there is just having the same functionality in some ways available in hardware description that you have in a general purpose programming language. Can you describe more concretely what Hardcaml is and how it works?
paragraphs.zh: 所以听起来其中一部分优势就是，在硬件描述中也能以某种方式获得通用编程语言中的同等功能。你能更具体地描述一下 Hardcaml 是什么以及它是如何工作的吗？

paragraphs.en: Hardcaml is basically an OCaml library. Now, technically, computer scientists would call it an embedded domain-specific language, or embedded DSL. So you have an OCaml host language, and in that host language, you write Hardcaml code, which is a description of hardware. And you know, the inputs to a Hardcaml design are bits and sets of bits representing your input signals. And the outputs are likewise sets of bits and bits, representing the outputs of your hardware. And out of all of this, you can generate VHDL or Verilog.
paragraphs.zh: Hardcaml 基本上是一个 OCaml 库。从技术角度来说，计算机科学家会称其为嵌入式领域特定语言，或嵌入式 DSL。你有一个 OCaml 宿主语言，在这个宿主语言中，你编写 Hardcaml 代码，这是对硬件的描述。你知道，Hardcaml 设计的输入是代表你输入信号的位和位集合。输出同样是位和位集合，代表你硬件的输出。从所有这些中，你可以生成 VHDL 或 Verilog。

paragraphs.en: And I think this is actually part of a more general phenomenon, which is, there are areas of technology where not having real first-class language support means that you're building up ad hoc systems on top of other languages, and in the process, you're building up a bunch of useful tools and abstractions. And maybe once, once enough of that has happened, you should roll those things into the language. But sometimes you get a lot out of just doing it in the library form.
paragraphs.zh: 我认为这实际上是一种更普遍现象的一部分——在某些技术领域，没有真正的一等语言支持意味着你在其他语言之上构建临时系统，在这个过程中积累了一堆有用的工具和抽象。也许一旦积累够了，就应该把这些东西整合进语言本身。但有时候仅仅以库的形式来做，你已经能获得很多好处。

paragraphs.en: I should give some of these like high-end hardware design tools their due. Languages like SystemVerilog are actually quite sophisticated: they have classes, they have templating, they have many features of a modern language. But they're not OCaml or Scala or Haskell.
paragraphs.zh: 我应该给那些高端硬件设计工具一些公平的评价。像 SystemVerilog 这样的语言实际上相当复杂：它们有类，有模板，有许多现代语言的特性。但它们不是 OCaml、Scala 或 Haskell。

paragraphs.en: So another thing that strikes me about the way in which you talk about Hardcaml and the advantages of using an actual general-purpose language for doing hardware design is that you talk about it as sort of liberating for the hardware designer. But here's something that strikes me: there are real hardware engineers who, you know, they use Verilog, they use SystemVerilog, and they don't seem to be suffering too greatly. It seems like somehow they get things done. Do you think there are ideas from software that they just haven't adopted yet? Or is the gap really not that big?
paragraphs.zh: 关于你谈论 Hardcaml 以及使用真正的通用语言进行硬件设计的优势的方式，还有一点让我印象深刻——你把它描述为对硬件设计师的一种解放。但有一点让我感到困惑：确实有些硬件工程师在使用 Verilog、使用 SystemVerilog，他们似乎也没有太大的痛苦。他们好像也能把事情做成。你认为有些软件中的理念他们还没有采用吗？还是说差距其实并没有那么大？

paragraphs.en: I think it's because I consider the abstraction level of designing hardware in Hardcaml to be the same as designing hardware in Verilog, just with a better language on top of it. I don't think of it as designing hardware at a higher level of abstraction. I think of it as designing hardware at the same level of abstraction.
paragraphs.zh: 我认为原因在于，我认为用 Hardcaml 设计硬件的抽象层级与用 Verilog 设计硬件是相同的，只是上面有一个更好的语言。我不认为这是在更高抽象层级上设计硬件，我认为这是在相同抽象层级上设计硬件，只是语言更好。

paragraphs.en: In defense of hardware engineers, I feel like there are lots of great ideas in software that take an agonizingly long time to get adopted. And hardware designers are trying to, at some level, trying to solve the problem of designing hardware. And the tools and idioms kind of build up around that, and they work, and people are kind of accustomed to them. And it takes something really compelling to make people change their workflows.
paragraphs.zh: 为硬件工程师辩护一下，我觉得软件中有很多很好的想法需要极其漫长的时间才能被采用。硬件设计师在某种程度上是在努力解决硬件设计的问题。工具和惯用法就围绕着这个目标建立起来，它们是有效的，人们也习惯了它们。要让人们改变工作流程，需要非常有说服力的理由。

paragraphs.en: Maybe, you're right. Maybe we're all just not trying hard enough.
paragraphs.zh: 也许你说得对。也许我们只是都没有足够努力地去尝试。

paragraphs.en: And the problem of coming up with these languages for hardware is a harder problem and has taken longer than in the software world. So there are options now. There's Bluespec Verilog. Can you talk about how Hardcaml is different from Bluespec?
paragraphs.zh: 而为硬件设计出这些语言的问题比软件世界中更难，也花了更长的时间。所以现在已经有了一些选择，比如 Bluespec Verilog。你能谈谈 Hardcaml 与 Bluespec 有什么不同吗？

paragraphs.en: Yeah, so I'll describe a little bit about how these systems work. So high level synthesis, basically the idea is that you write a description of a computation in some high level, sequential, program-like way. And then the high-level synthesis tool attempts to synthesize that into hardware. So the tool attempts to figure out the micro-architecture of the design, which means, for example, which operations happen at the same clock cycle and which operations might be pipelined and which might need some more complex scheduling.
paragraphs.zh: 好的，我来描述一下这些系统的工作原理。高级综合，基本思想是你以某种高层次、顺序的、类似程序的方式编写计算描述。然后高级综合工具尝试将其综合成硬件。所以该工具试图弄清楚设计的微架构，这意味着，例如，哪些操作发生在同一个时钟周期，哪些操作可能需要流水线化，哪些可能需要更复杂的调度。

paragraphs.en: So maybe a way of describing the difference is that Hardcaml is built around a little core calculus that's basically just combinational logic and registers, right? And so when you write hardware in Hardcaml, you're directly specifying the micro-architecture of your design, the same way that you would if you were writing Verilog or VHDL.
paragraphs.zh: 所以描述这个区别的一种方式是：Hardcaml 围绕一个小型核心演算构建，这个演算基本上只是组合逻辑和寄存器，对吧？所以当你用 Hardcaml 编写硬件时，你是在直接指定设计的微架构，就像你写 Verilog 或 VHDL 时一样。

paragraphs.en: I'm guessing here a little bit. I have not written a lot of Bluespec. I've mainly read papers on it. I think Bluespec is more like a high level synthesis tool in that the model you write in is higher level, and it infers or generates the micro-architecture for you.
paragraphs.zh: 我这里有些是在猜测。我没有写过很多 Bluespec，主要是读过相关论文。我认为 Bluespec 更像是一个高级综合工具，因为你编写的模型层级更高，它会为你推断或生成微架构。

paragraphs.en: So are they any advantages that you see of the approach that Hardcaml takes over the Bluespec approach?
paragraphs.zh: 那么你认为 Hardcaml 的方法相比 Bluespec 的方法有什么优势吗？

paragraphs.en: I think even designers using Bluespec would have a language like Verilog or Hardcaml for the cases where they want to design at the RTL level. There are things that, you know, if you're building something really performance sensitive, like a network processor, for example, you really want to control the micro-architecture very closely. And so having a high level synthesis approach there just won't give you what you need.
paragraphs.zh: 我认为即使是使用 Bluespec 的设计师，在想要进行 RTL 级别设计时，也会需要像 Verilog 或 Hardcaml 这样的语言。有些情况下，比如你在构建对性能非常敏感的东西，例如网络处理器，你真的需要非常精密地控制微架构。在这种情况下，高级综合方法就无法给你所需要的东西。

paragraphs.en: Here's another language related question about all of this. If you look at the world of hardware description, there seems to be no widely adopted replacement for Verilog or VHDL, even though people have been trying. Why do you think that's the case?
paragraphs.zh: 关于这一切，还有另一个与语言相关的问题。如果你看看硬件描述的世界，似乎即使人们一直在尝试，Verilog 或 VHDL 也没有被广泛采用的替代品。你认为这是为什么？

paragraphs.en: No.
paragraphs.zh: 没有。

paragraphs.en: So there's a library in Scala called Chisel which has similar goals and aspirations. There's also a library in Haskell called Clash. These are both reasonable approaches but neither of them have any serious traction in the hardware industry right now. There's also Bluespec which we talked about. It's interesting in that it has some commercial backing. But I think in the end, a language that is going to win has to be good at all levels: it has to work well at the RTL level, so people can use it as a drop in replacement for Verilog or VHDL; it has to be a good host language; and it has to work well for high level synthesis.
paragraphs.zh: Scala 中有一个叫 Chisel 的库，有着类似的目标和抱负。Haskell 中也有一个叫 Clash 的库。这两种方法都是合理的，但目前在硬件行业都没有得到真正的认可。还有我们谈到的 Bluespec，有趣的是它有一些商业支持。但我认为，最终能胜出的语言必须在各个层面都表现出色：它必须在 RTL 层面运行良好，让人们能够用它直接替换 Verilog 或 VHDL；它必须是一个好的宿主语言；而且它必须在高级综合方面也表现良好。

paragraphs.en: They're both software systems. Both, I think, are better approaches to designing hardware than trying to do it in raw Verilog. But neither of them, I think, are the right long-term solution.
paragraphs.zh: 它们都是软件系统。我认为，与直接用原始 Verilog 设计硬件相比，两者都是更好的方法。但我认为它们都不是正确的长期解决方案。

paragraphs.en: So a thing you mentioned along the way there is that there's not yet a university churning out Hardcaml engineers. And I want to ask a related question, which is, you know, do you actually want other people to use Hardcaml? You've open sourced it, which is sort of a prerequisite for that. But is there an active effort to try and get people to use it?
paragraphs.zh: 你在其中提到了一点，那就是目前还没有大学在批量培养 Hardcaml 工程师。我想问一个相关的问题：你真的希望其他人使用 Hardcaml 吗？你已经开源了它，这在某种程度上是一个前提。但有没有积极的努力来推动人们使用它？

paragraphs.en: Well, I would like people to use it for sure. I think it's gonna be hard to get people to use it. Over the years, I've made it as polished as I reasonably can, and it is open source. So maybe some people find it and try it. But practically speaking, it's not like we're doing a marketing campaign or trying to get universities to teach it.
paragraphs.zh: 嗯，我当然希望人们使用它。我认为让人们使用它会很难。这些年来，我在合理范围内把它打磨得尽可能精良，而且它是开源的。所以也许有些人会发现它并尝试使用。但实际上，我们并没有进行什么营销活动，也没有试图让大学去教它。

paragraphs.en: And most of the stuff that we build internally is just not stuff that's of general interest. So it's very hard for us to produce examples that demonstrate how you could use Hardcaml for something cool that other people want to do.
paragraphs.zh: 而且我们内部构建的大多数东西，都不是具有普遍意义的东西。所以对我们来说，很难提供示例来展示如何用 Hardcaml 来做其他人也想做的有意思的事情。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, unfortunately, I think that's true.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，遗憾的是，我认为确实如此。

paragraphs.en: We've talked some about how hardware can be useful more generally. How does hardware come up, and what role does it play at Jane Street specifically?
paragraphs.zh: 我们已经聊了一些关于硬件在更广泛层面如何发挥作用的话题。那么硬件在 Jane Street 具体是怎么出现的，又扮演着什么角色？

paragraphs.en: So really, the focus for us is around network packet processing. I'd be surprised if we end up writing a general purpose trading algorithm in hardware. I think that's a step too far. It's actually quite hard to trade well even in software. You know, our trading teams have developed deep understanding of how to trade well.
paragraphs.zh: 所以说，我们的重点其实是网络数据包处理。如果我们最终在硬件中编写通用交易算法，我会很惊讶。我认为那走得太远了。其实即使在软件中交易做好也相当困难。你知道，我们的交易团队对如何做好交易有深刻的理解。

paragraphs.en: And I think the background fact about trading that justifies all this is that trading systems have to process, you know, tens of thousands of market events per second, and you need to do that at microsecond latencies. That's pretty demanding.
paragraphs.zh: 我认为关于交易的背景事实——也是支撑这一切的理由——是交易系统必须每秒处理数万个市场事件，而且需要在微秒级延迟下完成。这要求相当高。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, and the problem's worse than that, right? Because you're not connected to one of these data sources, you're connected to many of them. And you have to do the same kind of processing on all of them, and process them all simultaneously. So you really do need to be able to process a lot of data at very high speeds and low latencies.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，而且问题比这还要复杂，对吧？因为你连接的不是一个这样的数据源，而是很多个。你必须对所有这些数据源做同样的处理，并且同时处理它们。所以你真的需要能够以非常高的速度和低延迟处理大量数据。

paragraphs.en: One of the magic tricks here of hardware is that the determinism is such that if you understand the latency of one packet going through your system, you know the latency of every packet going through your system.
paragraphs.zh: 硬件在这里的一个神奇之处在于，它的确定性使得如果你了解一个数据包通过你系统的延迟，你就知道了每个数据包通过你系统的延迟。

paragraphs.en: Yeah. So like when we build our tests for these systems, we basically test them always at line rate, right? Because, you know, if it's a 100 gigabit or 400 gigabit input, you test it by running 100 gigabit or 400 gigabit into it. And when a hardware design passes that test, it will always pass that test, because it's deterministic. And so, you know, so that kind of gives you a lot of confidence in a way that's very different from software.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。所以当我们为这些系统构建测试时，我们基本上总是以线速进行测试，对吧？因为你知道，如果是 100 吉比特或 400 吉比特的输入，你就通过向它输入 100 吉比特或 400 吉比特来测试。而当一个硬件设计通过了这个测试，它就会永远通过这个测试，因为它是确定的。所以，这以一种与软件非常不同的方式给了你很大的信心。

paragraphs.en: So I guess at this point I just want to thank you for joining me. This has been a really fun conversation. I've learned a lot. I hope our listeners have as well.
paragraphs.zh: 那么在这一点上，我只想感谢你加入我们。这真的是一次非常有趣的对话。我学到了很多，希望我们的听众也是如此。

paragraphs.en: My pleasure.
paragraphs.zh: 我的荣幸。

paragraphs.en: You can find links to some of the things we talked about, as well as a full transcript of the episode, on our website at signalsandthreads.com. If you have thoughts on the episode or ideas for future episodes, please reach out to us at podcasts@janestreet.com.
paragraphs.zh: 你可以在我们的网站 signalsandthreads.com 上找到我们谈到的一些内容的链接，以及本期节目的完整文字稿。如果你对本期节目有想法，或者对未来节目有建议，请通过 podcasts@janestreet.com 联系我们。
